title: Causes of World War I
description: Test your understanding of what caused World War I.

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** true of the European empires before World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
It is *not* true that the European powers had little to no influence in Asia and the Pacific. 

The European powers had colonial possession throughout the world, including Asia and the Pacific. The Indian Subcontinent was under the control of the British.  Many of the European powers were competing for influence in China (e.g., British controlled Hong Kong and Germans controlled Tsing Tao in China).  The Dutch and French had significant possessions in Indonesia and South East Asia, respectively.

This is covered in [this tutorial](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-tutorial/v/empires-before-world-war-i).

choices.content: Italy and Germany had each been unified states for less than 50 years
Great Britain, France, Italy and Germany each had possessions in Africa
Germany had a possession in China
The European powers had little to no influence in Asia and the Pacific

assessment_items.question_content:
Which was **not** a likely driving factor for Archduke Franz Ferdinand's assassins in Sarajevo in 1914?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Topic covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-tutorial/v/assassination-of-franz-ferdinand-by-gavrilo-princip). 

Anger over Serbia being part of Yugoslavia was **not** a likely driving factor for Archduke Franz Ferdinand's assassins in Sarajevo in 1914

There were aspirations amongst Serbians and other in the Balkans to unify the "Southern Slavic" people into one future state of Greater Serbia or Yugoslavia (which didn't exist at the time).  This was the stated goal of the secret Serbian military society known as the Black Hand and its related youth movement (the assassins were part of this movement, which was called Young Bosnia).

Slovenia and Croatia, however, were already part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire.  On top of that, Austria formally annexed Bosnia in 1908, which only further angered the Yugoslav movement.

choices.content: Anger over Austria's formal annexation of Bosnia
A desire to free Croatia and Bosnia from Austrian rule
Aspirations of forming a unified  "south-slavic"
Anger over Serbia being part of Yugoslavia

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** true about the people of Croatia, Bosnia and Serbia?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is covered [in this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-tutorial/v/language-and-religion-of-the-former-yugoslavia).

They all speak variations of Serbo-Croatian which is a Slavic language and, thus, have strong linguistic ties.  Because of these ties, they are unified into Yugoslavia ("Yugo Slav" literally means "Southern Slav").

The people of Croatia are predominantly Roman Catholic.  Serbians are predominantly Eastern Orthodox.  Bosnia is nearly half Muslim (Bosniaks), but also has large populations of Eastern Orthodox (Bosnian Serb) and Roman Catholic Christians (Bosnian Croat).
They definitely do not have close religious ties.  Their religious division would be a cause of major conflict in the late 20th century.

choices.content: They share close religious ties
They would have their own state of Yugoslavia after World War I
They have strong linguistic ties
Croatians are primarily Roman Catholic and Serbians are primarily Eastern Orthodox
Croatia and Bosnia were part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire during World War I and the Empire overran Serbia during the War.

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** generally considered a factor that led to the start of World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is covered in [this tutorial](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-tutorial/v/empires-before-world-war-i).


The direct cause of the War is generally considered to be the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, which led to Austria declaring war on Serbia.  This caused Russia to mobilize in Serbia's defense, which gave Germany the pretext for declaring war on Russia and France. Germany's invasion of Belgium on its way to France gave Britain the justification to declare war on Germany.    The domino of events was made possible by the various alliances (most of which were created in the late 1800s) among the powers and the significant arms buildup leading up to 1914.

Tsar Nicholas II was not overthrown in Russia until early 1917 and the Bolsheviks led by Lenin did not come to power until November of that year.  This played a role in Russia exiting the war but did not catalyze the war (which started in 1914).

choices.content: The various alliances among the major powers prior to the start of the War
The arms race between Germany and the United Kingdom leading up to the War
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria in Sarajevo by groups associated with Serbia
Russian mobilization of its army in defense of Serbia once Austria had declared war
The rise of the Bolsheviks in Imperial Russia

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false: Austria's reaction to Archduke Franz Ferdinand's assassination in June of 1914 was to place an ultimatum on Serbia to bring everyone involved to justice. Serbia tried to comply, but their efforts were not considered sufficient.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is true and covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-tutorial/v/the-great-war-begins). 

Austria's reaction to Archduke Franz Ferdinand's assassination in June of 1914 did place an ultimatum on Serbia to bring everyone involved to justice. Serbia tried to comply, but their efforts were not considered sufficient.

Many historians question whether war could have been avoided even if Serbia were able to somehow meet Austria's nearly impossible demands.

choices.content: true
false

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true about the major empires leading up to World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
By definition, an empire is variety of states and people (often times with very different languages and religions) that were unified under a common ruler (a king emperor or sultan).

For example, the British Empire at the time included India and much of Africa (both of which themselves have many different ethnic groups and religions). Therefore, this answer is not true: Most of the empires were unified by their common language and religions

This is discussed in more detail [in this video on the empires before World War I](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-tutorial/v/empires-before-world-war-i).

choices.content: The empires of Europe contained people of many ethnicities and religions
The major powers were in a race to build their empires which added to their wealth and prestige
The United Kingdom had one of the most extensive empires (arguably the most extensive) in large part due to its dominant navy
Germany was a new "empire" and was eager to expand beyond its current holdings in Africa and the Pacific
Most of the empires were unified by their common language and religions

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false: Russia's invasion of Austria in defense of Serbia was the justification for Germany declaring war on Russia in 1914. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The answer is false. Russia did not invade Austria prior to Germany's declaration of war in 1914.  Russia did however begin mobilizing its army in defense of Serbia (after Austria declared war on Serbia). Germany, knowing that it would have to act swiftly in a likely two-front war with both France and Russia, used Russia's mobilization as justification to declare war on it and France.

This is covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-tutorial/v/the-great-war-begins).

choices.content: true
false

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is true about the European alliances in years before World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-tutorial/v/alliances-leading-to-world-war-i).

France was allied with Russia in years before World War I. 

In fact, the Franco-Russian Military Convention of 1892 called for military assistance both ways in the event of a third party attack on one of them.  The 1904 Entente Cordial (between the UK and France) and the 1907 Anglo-Russian Convention established good relations between the UK, France and Russia forming the Triple Entente.

choices.content: Great Britain had an alliance with Austria-Hungary
Russia had an alliance with the Ottoman empire
France was allied with Russia
Germany was allied with Great Britain

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of these events or facts provides the best justification for blaming Germany for World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Discussed in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/ww1-aftermath/v/paris-peace-conference-and-treaty-of-versailles).

It is completely debatable whether Germany should have been assigned full "war guilt" as the Treaty of Versailles later did.  One could argue that the Austrians were overly aggressive in their ultimatum to the Serbians (and their later declaration of War).  The Russians also began mobilizing in defense of Serbia before Germany had declared war.
The Zimmerman Telegram helped instigate American entry into the War in 1917 but did not trigger the war itself.

Bosnia was annexed by the Austrians.  One could argue that this helped precipitate the assassination of Franz Ferdinand and thus helped trigger the war.  The Germans, however, had nothing to do with this.
The Anglo-German arms race (both on sea and land) helped make the environment ripe for an all-out war between these two powers.  However, the British were as complicit in this as the Germans.

The existence of the Schlieffen Plan by itself did not lead to the war. Germany quickly declared war on Russian and France only days after the Austro-Hungarians declared war on Serbia.  They used Russian mobilization as a pretext, but one could argue that they needlessly escalated what would have been a regional conflict in the Balkans to a pan-European (and eventually much of the world) conflict involving the major powers.  The speed with which they were able to roll into Belgium indicates that they were merely looking for a trigger to execute on their Schlieffen Plan.  

So, the speed and aggression with which the Germans engaged the French and Russians shortly after Austria's declaration of war provides the best justification for blaming Germany for World War I.

choices.content: the Zimmerman Telegram
the annexation of Bosnia
the Anglo-German naval race
the existence of the Schlieffen Plan prior to the War
the speed and aggression with which the Germans engaged the French and Russians shortly after Austria's declaration of war
